title: Quiz: scaling reads on a read-heavy app
prompt_md: Your app is read-heavy — far more reads than writes — and the single database is struggling. What is the common way to scale the reads?
options: Upgrade to a bigger single machine forever as traffic grows
Shard the database across many nodes immediately, since that always scales best
Add read replicas and route read queries to them while writes go to the primary, usually combined with caching — keeping replication lag in mind
Move all reads to the application's memory and stop using the database for queries
explanation_md: For a **read-heavy** workload the standard move is **read replicas**: copies of the primary that take over read traffic so the **primary** is freed to handle **writes**. You route reads to replicas and writes to the primary, and layer a **cache** (e.g. Redis) in front to absorb the hottest reads — together this is cheap and low-risk. The one caveat to name is **replication lag**: a replica can briefly serve slightly stale data, so a read immediately after a write may not reflect it (**eventual consistency**). **Sharding** is heavier and aimed at write/size limits, and vertical scale-up hits a ceiling.
